sentences.text: Shall I continue?
Where were I?
Yeah.
We thought that winter is coming.
And we have a chance.
We have to open this all.
And everyone has to go out.
And we have to bring in potatoes as much we can.
So we cleaned through this [INAUDIBLE].
The, the first one, they filled it up.
Which this was for us a blessing what they did.
And we filled it.
We open it.
And we went out, 12 guys and bags.
As you come on the field, there was potatoes all over.
Used to-- come and say, good evening.
Nobody was there, but just in case somebody is going to answer.
But we [INAUDIBLE] that evening and load the potatoes into the cave.
Come to the hole, just empty it.
The ki-- the young boys inside took it away.
And we were like till the morning.
And I come in, inside.
I see a mountain of potatoes.
And we did the same thing.
We put in the frame.
And we covered this up, the same thing.
We come the next day.
Everything is inside.
They discovered us again.
We had no choice.
We had to go.
But-- so we brought in the potatoes.
And we-- [DRINKS] there was corn in the fields.
I used to bring corn.
And, and it was going on like that.
And winter's coming.
I say, winter is coming.
We have to prepare ourselves.
I said, I thought it would be good to have a Sleigh.
You know, a sleigh would be good.
Because there going to be snow.
So but the sleight, how the sleigh is going to go out through there.
It has to be a sleigh from parts to assemble it when we go out.
So I brought in from the forest some young saplings, young little trees.
And I cut them like that, four by five, two lengths about five, six feet long.
And I made it place like what the-- what to bend to make it thinner.
And I cooked it in the hot water there, cooked that-- the one side.
And I had there a board about 12 inches.
I made it round.
And I tied down with a piece of wire on one end.
And when I cooked, I just bend it.
And I let it stay for a day or two.
And I come out.
I says, I have only on.
I made another one like that.
Then I build that sleigh, like a sleigh you put horizontal, vert-- you know, this. put it this.
But I didn't have no nails.
So everything has to be with made like it has to made like you put it in.
It's going to stay together.
So one side and then the other side.
And then this way, put together when we went out I took a part, my brother a part, this guy a part, come outside, put together the sleigh.
Sleigh is ready and strong-- put a few hundred pounds on the seat.
So we had a sleigh.
The once my brother went to the-- went to Bilcze Zlote.
He used to go-- my brother used to go-- and used to sleep there in a barn.
He used to have a good, a friend there, OK?
And his name was [INAUDIBLE], Sam Panki.
And he-- when he used to come to him, right away he brought him up some bread, milk.
He knew that he's there.
And my brother used to make arrangements.
So one day he comes and tells me-- comes back at night about 11 o'clock.
He says, take the sleigh.
I-- we have to go back to [INAUDIBLE] and bring some grain.
So I-- right away, I take the sleigh and we walking there.
We brought up the grain from the-- because Bilcze Zlote was in a valley.
He brought up the grain.
And he start pulling.
And he pulled.
And as he pulled-- not far from the forest [INAUDIBLE].
Say Listen, it's daylight!
It's going to be daylight soon.
What are we going to do?
He says, no.
I say, you remember.
You came here.
We went back.
Is it.
And as we talk comes daylight.
What do we do with the grain?
Is dangerous.
And in the-- in, in the left side, there were four farmers.
We knew all of them.
He says, let's leave the grain here.
We see on the field some old straw.
And we cover up the grain and go down to the cave.
And as we come in in the forest there, our forest there, and we walk.
And a guy, Ukrainian guy, comes in the front of us.
And he asked my br-- asked us, where are you going?
My brother says, I'm going to the meal.
And I didn't see Nissen for eight months during daylight.
And I turn around like that.
And I look at this guy.
I got scared.
He was yellow, [INAUDIBLE].
No--
A little cap with two little a-- like a little cap.
And I see a sweater full of mud, frozen mud, frozen mud, pants.
I look at him.
I said to myself, I must look the same thing.
He looked terrible, like a wild man, but very strong.
And he came to the cave.
And Sam, my brother Sam, [?
Shlomo, ?] he was there waiting for us.
Because we used to close at night, used to put in some stones in the hole.
And he almost close the cave, because he thought maybe we slept outside.
And we came in.
And we said to my mother, asking, where's the grain?
We says, we left it, [INAUDIBLE].
We didn't want to tell her that we left this on the field, something like that.
And I sleep with my brother.
And I says, oh, we will see.
Tomorrow, we going to find that grain.
Ten o'clock at night or something, we go there.
It was winter time.
Was before Christmas time.
I left out some thing.
I think I should stop with this year-- oh, finish this story.
Then I go back, because I have to go back to, to October.
This is already and December.
And we come there.
The grain is not there.
We see that horses and a sleigh were there.
See, it was so danger, we had the sleigh.
Somebody could [INAUDIBLE] to kill us.
But we didn't have a choice.
We have to go and bring the grain.
My mother, little sisters, everybody has to eat.
We have to bring this.
And we see the grain is gone.
What shall we do?
My brother said, we going to go and see where the grain went.
And he's going where exactly where the sleigh.
And we see it went to the farmers, the four farmers.
He comes in to the first, knocks on the door.
[INAUDIBLE]-- was his name, [INAUDIBLE].
He says, no I didn't take this.
And my brother said, listen.
You know that we-- you have what to eat.
You sleep in a warm bed.
We haven't got what to eat.
And if we are not going to have what to eat, you're not going to eat, too.
The guy got a little bit scared.
He says, oh, go.
There is one bag.
He gives a bag.
Where the rest?
He says, there, there, to the other farmers.
We picked up all the stuff.
But in meantime, but four or five hours-- we were supposed to be back in half an hour.
So we came back.
My mother says and this she was sure that we both lost-- she lost both, that we are gone.
Because it's almost impossible not to be back in half an hour from the, from this place.
Anyway, we got back the grain.
But to go back a little bit like to November-- on the tenth of November, 1943, me and my brother, Nissen, and my father, went to Bilcze Zlote.
We had there the same guy there, his brothers.
And somehow-- I don't know where Nissen got some stuff-- he bought from them about 250 kilos of grain, different things.
This was because it was almost winter.
And this was for us a very, you know, a very big thing, and we had to sleep over.
And at, at night, he would bring us the grain to the forest.
And from there, we would carry it to the cave.
We said to our father, go back to the cave.
What are you going sit here?
Go back to the cave.
My brother goes-- my father goes back to cave.
About two in the morning, father is back.
He say, what are you doing?
He says, something is wrong with the cave as I walked to the cave, somebody was crying, yelling, [INAUDIBLE] because I'm going to shoot.
You know, we-- we knew that something sad was there.
He says, the family is there.
We are here.
We wouldn't be able to come in.
They were-- how are they going to survive?
What's going to happen?
Had no choice.
So father sleep here with us.
At night, about 10 o'clock, the farmer put the grain on the wagon.
I was working the side like-- the village, nobody should catch me there.
And the farmer brother stood for the, the grain.
And we left our father with the grain.
And we are going to the cave.
And we come to the wall through the forest.
And it was on the 11th of November.
And it was-- the snow start to fall snow.
And we come.
And we lie down on our stomachs.
And we started crawling to the cave.
I said to my brother, why should we crawl both?
Stay here
Or he told me.
I, I, I don't want to stay.
They'll have to kill two.
They kill one is enough.
I told him even different.
I told him, listen, if they kill me, you still got to do something.
But if they kill you, the game is over.
So I was crawling in my stomach.
And I come to the cave.
And I look down, see it's quiet.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I just lie down.
And Sam was there.
My brother Sam says, everything is all right.
Nothing happened.
Yesterday, I was over [INAUDIBLE] with [INAUDIBLE].
And he thought that some, some [?
Ukrainian ?] comes there.
So he was yelling.
He find that my father dropped there the-- his bag what he had.
And what he had in his bag?
He had there towels.
And he had a [INAUDIBLE], So [INAUDIBLE] brother chase away the father.
So we went back to the father.
Everything is fine now, right?
And we start carrying the brother, father.
Father, we didn't let our father to carry some stuff.
We brought in the stuff one time and even back.
And we brought in the grain.
And we brought in the grain.
I went in first.
And my brother-- listen, he carried a big bag.
And somehow in that chimney, this-- it got stuck.
He couldn't-- not out, not in.
In the meantime, there was about 10 [INAUDIBLE] people, from our people.
Because if everything is clear, nobody will leave.
So they were waiting to go out.
Suddenly, I hear-- some noise on the top.
I say, who is outside?
Everybody-- everybody's in.
And we hear pow-- they start shooting.
Pow-- they start shooting-- once, twice, again.
The [?
Ukrainian ?] police came at night.
And it was like two minutes-- not, not five but two minutes.
They would have caught me, and my brother, my father, or the other people.
My brother wouldn't be stuck with this, with this thing.
The other people go out.
So this was a miracle once.
But my-- so what we did, we closed up this entrance.
Everybody is inside.
We got the grain.
We clo-- we packed it with stones.
And we didn't go out for six week.
The meantime, winter is coming.
There is a lot of snow.
And it filled up everything.
And the police, every day they came there.
Because after we got out, [INAUDIBLE] told us.
Every day, they were standing there at night.
He used to come.
And they see, hey, those guys are not coming out.
They must have some more, some more exit.
Because they made in the other cave.
Because they knew this is the same people they made before here.
They have some more.
What, we going to stay here like dummies?
Because after, we knew that they were talking, that we had, in the forest one, we have in some other places.
This was, this was-- we survive because they, they didn't knew the secret that we didn't have no other exit.
We had to go through here.
So when we came out, this, this [INAUDIBLE] says, what's happened?
Yesterday were police there.
We just got out in time.
But before,
It was the four my-- I'll tell a-- I have to tell about my, my sister, Chana.
She was not with us in the cave when we came in this cave.
And she was in the first cave.
But in the second cave, she had-- there's a woman that she, that she kept her there.
And she used to work for her there.
She was very-- she had very good there.
Yeah, after-- [INAUDIBLE] happened.
Once my brother comes from the village-- because my sister was hiding in Bilcze Zlote.
This was before Rosh Hashanah.
This was sometime in September, 1943.
My brother, Nissen, comes from the village.
And he says that he-- in the village, somebody told him-- this Sam-- that they talk in the village that my sister Chana is hiding in this place.
He comes and tells this to my mother.
My mother says, what?
Tomorrow night, you're going to bring it here right away.
Oh, well, they, they told that she is dead already.
They said, police are going to come.
The next night, it was, it was late in October.
No, it was not late.
Because it was mud-- it was-- the field was a lot, rains and was bad to walk.
Me and my brother, Nissen, we were walking about 10 kilometers in mud like that.
And Every came over the village.
And he knew exactly, my brother, where she is, what house.
We down the mountains, we came down.
Didn't knock on the door, just forced the window open.
And we came in the house.
And in the room-- shh, don't talk!
We are Chana's brothers.
And my br-- my sister came out.
She was so-- she looked so good.
Because we were in cave and she was dressed clean.
And she was fat, nice.
I say, Chana, they know that you are here.
You can't stay here, because they'll catch you. you have to come with us.
That woman didn't want to let her go, she says.
Tell Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE] we taking her only for a day.
We'll bring her back.
But the mother is not feeling good.
You bring her back.
We took Chana with us.
After we initially came to the, to the village, three or four days later, they told them.
They, they weren't-- the police came to that [INAUDIBLE].
And they took apart the house.
They-- the barns, they were, they were looking for Chana.
If we wouldn't take her at that time, she sure wouldn't be alive.
So this was in, this was-- and Chana came before the holidays.
And she says to my mother, I fast already.
She see the-- she took the, the holidays like the calendar from last year.
Anyway, it came Yontif.
It came Yom Kippur.
We were all fasting.
And we didn't sleep the-- in Kippur, the day.
We prayed all day.
My fa-- my brother-in-law, [INAUDIBLE], he was educated good in Yiddish and [INAUDIBLE] master.
And he was praying all day.
We were crying and praying all day.
And we didn't eat nothing.
We prayed all [INAUDIBLE], too.
And then at night, we fast.
So we had those ducks and those, those good things to eat.
This was, this was this time.
Now, now in the winter.
We're coming to 1944.
Now, yeah, Now, we arrive 1944, exactly.
Now I-- in 1944 in February, my brother arranged for a few hundred kilos, maybe two or three hundred kilos, grain.
He, he got some stuff took back from some farmers [INAUDIBLE].
And he says, OK, we are going to bring this here.
We took the, the, the sleigh because of winter.
Then we left the sleigh on the top, like on the mountain.
And we went down to bring out the grain.
We bring one.
We brought one load.
Three times, we load it up.
And we tried to pull it.
We can't even move it.
We thought that we can move away this house.
Can't move it.
What we going to do?
My brother says, I'm going to go steal a horse.
I said, we, we going to steal a horse?
They kill us when we are there.
He says, I'm going to steal a horse.
So we go.
So he went like down to the village for the horse.
And I was lying in the sleigh.
And I was watching [INAUDIBLE].
Suddenly, I hear a horse-- snort, snort.
Sound like horse.
I look around.
I see he is on horse.
He's coming on a horse.
Comes over with a beautiful, nice horse.
Say, Nissen, why you forgot?
You forgot a harness?
How we going to pull the harness?
He says, I'm going back for the harness.
I say, no.
Hold it.
You are not going back for the harness.
Hold it.
We're going to make a harness.
And you remember, I told you that we were wearing those Ukrainian shirts and pants?
Say, how you going to make a harness?
I say, hold it.
Let's undress.
We took of our [INAUDIBLE].
They are those, those [INAUDIBLE] thing.
And we undressed completely naked.
I took off my pants and my shirt.
He took off his.
We put back the rags.
And we tied down.
We made a harness.
Tied on the, the horse and made a harness, tied on good.
I was jump-- I sit on the horse.
And he was lying down on the, on the sleigh.
And in one hour, you were the cave with all the grain.
We brought everything in.
But we had-- we didn't kill the horse.
Because we didn't eat-- what you mean, you're going to eat the horse meat?
[INAUDIBLE] people eat horse meat.
So we let the horse go.
And this was in February.
Now it comes closer, comes March.
Once I want to go.
We have to go for food and it start-- snow start melting.
And it comes in [INAUDIBLE] we come to the, to the entrance to go out.
We can't go out.
The bars of water coming in, because this place was very low-- water one day, next day.
Anyway, we find a way out of the hole.
We took out our clothes.
We put them in a bag.
One, one went out with a rope and just pulled it through fast.
And we got dressed outside.
[INAUDIBLE] are not bad.
As we go out, it was pink already-- nice March.
My brother-- hey, wait a minute.
It's cannons-- because he was in the army.
Boom.
He says, it must be close.
The, the, the front must be close [INAUDIBLE].
The, the Red Army and the Germans, they must fight close by already.
We see fires.
And we go into the [INAUDIBLE].
And he says, yes.
The Germans are retreating.
They're going back already.
Already for a few days, they going back.
And, and think the Russians are going to be soon.
We came back running back.
They say there's going to be liberation soon.
And we came back.
And then we go out again.
A few days later, we go out.
And we go into a guy, to the same [INAUDIBLE].
I see-- I was outside.
My brother [INAUDIBLE].
They made the lights inside.
Always was dark.
And see they're jumping, you see.
I go, what's happened?
The, the Red Army is already here, already for four days here in the [INAUDIBLE] in the town.
We are, we are liberated!
So we got out next day.
What date was this again?
This was in March.
This was about the end of the-- by the end of March.
And we got out.
And the Red Army was here.
There are soldiers.
And the-- all the people are inside, only a few men.
And suddenly, the-- they tell us, you are, we are going.
We have to run away, because there is a whole army of Germans.
We just got them out.
They all have to go through.
Your better come with us or-- we are not going to go away.
We came back to the cave.
And we were in the cave about two weeks more.
And it came such a big storm.
It covered us up.
And we went-- tried to go out.
We, we got out.
And we went to that [INAUDIBLE].
The Germans are still going.
But there was a Jewish guy, was like, like, he was like a Gentile working for a guy.
And we made with him a, like a, a understanding that if the Russians are going to be here, you should come and take a bottle and put it on a piece of paper and write us down what happens.
One day, we coming and see a bottle is inside.
Took out the bottle and read.
The Russians came in Wednesday, this and this.
They, they they are here.
You can go out.
So this was April.
On the 12th of April, we all start crawling out.
When?
Now, there was, there was 38 people and eight children.
From two till 12.
No, no.
Then they can't-- nobody-- my mother, my sister, with kids, never.
They never.
A lot of people never went out, only, only maybe a dozen or two.
That's all.
This was in the 12th of April.
We crawled out.
My brother-in-law, [INAUDIBLE], he was a very capable man and a very smart one.
He brought at Russian officer there to the cave, because just in case something shouldn't happen to us.
And we came out.
Now, now, where are we-- where are we going now?
Yeah.
The Russians were there.
What was your feeling at the time?
It was-- I am.
I, I'll be able to go during the day?
How, how is that possible?
And I thought, hey, it's Pesach, no?
My mother may be is going to something for Pesach, too.
I, right away, I thought of it.
And we start going to-- we out.
Where we going?
We're going to Korolowka, to our town.
And we walking.
I remember it well.
With my brother and [INAUDIBLE], we walk first.
And we came in to the city.
Most of houses were, were demolished.
You see a chimney sticks out here.
We go to see where is house?
Oh, here are the steps.
I recognize that.
No houses, no barns, no nothing.
So we came out.
He says, we're going to have to find a house.
So we find a house, a Jewish house, a good house.
And we set up there.
This was April.
And we were there.
And, and we didn't know what danger it was there.
It was very danger.
We were there.
And once a Red Army went through.
And my sister and another girl there, they was standing there.
And he says, we are stationed at this place.
Come at night.
Take your brother and bring her back.
I have to give you something.
We gone there.
He takes out a, a semi-automatic rifle with a gun.
He says, now, take this here.
Put this in the bag.
You are still in danger.
You-- when you sleep at night, somebody should stay by the window with this here.
We didn't even realize that this is-- that we are liberated.
Because there was still Nazis here.
There was still Ukrainian police hiding here and there.
So we did this here.
And we were there for about four weeks.
Because on the-- on April-- on, on May the 20th, the Russian, the Russians, all the young people had to go to the army.
I didn't go.
I, I had time to go.
My brother had a rifle already.
He was working for the police when they tried to [INAUDIBLE] for the police in order not to go in the army.
Any event, with just friends, [INAUDIBLE] to a guy that he was-- his name was [INAUDIBLE].
He went with me to school.
He became SS.
And he came to this house.
And they find him in bed.
And the, the police-- the chief and the police, they are from the, the Russian.
He says, don't blame me.
[INAUDIBLE] where we were left to find.
And we find ourselves.
So they came there and [INAUDIBLE].
He says, I am sick.
Then he open the basement and full basement of Jewish stuff, all Jewish things-- coats and suits and things.
So this-- then my brother had the auto-- automatic rifle with him.
And this-- he also was in the cave with us, went for a wagon.
In the meantime, this [INAUDIBLE] curl up on the bed.
And my brother stays with the rifle.
We hear pow!
He sees blood already.
[INAUDIBLE] came out.
He took a rifle and want to shoot my brother.
And if my brother wouldn't have the automatic rifle which had like a disk-- it hit the disk and this.
And it his finger.
And then he--
